These churches were in such different states regarding the purity of their teachings and the strength of their devotion that the words of Christ spoken to them will always apply to other churches and believers. Christ knows and observes their condition; even though he is in heaven, he walks among his churches on earth, noticing what is wrong and what they need. The church of Ephesus is commended for its diligence in service. Christ keeps track of every hour his servants work for him, and their labor will not be wasted in the Lord. But it is not enough to be diligent; there must also be patience in enduring hardship, and patience in waiting. And though we should show gentleness to everyone, we must also demonstrate strong opposition to their sins. The sin Christ charged this church with was not abandoning love, but losing the intense devotion that had initially been present. Christ is displeased with his people when he sees them become less enthusiastic and cold toward him. Surely this mention in Scripture of Christians forsaking their first love rebukes those who speak of it carelessly, thus attempting to excuse indifference and laziness in themselves and others; our Savior considers such indifference sinful. They must repent: they must feel sorrow and shame for their decline and humbly confess it to God. They must strive to regain their original zeal, sensitivity, and seriousness, and should pray earnestly and watch diligently as they did when they first began following God. If the grace and Spirit of Christ are disregarded, we may expect his displeasure. A positive observation is made about what was good among them. Indifference toward truth and error, good and evil, may be called charity and meekness, but it is not so; and it displeases Christ. The Christian life is a battle against sin, Satan, the world, and our own desires. We must never give in to our spiritual enemies, and then we will experience a glorious victory and reward. All who persevere will receive from Christ, as from the Tree of Life, perfection and confirmation in holiness and happiness, not in the earthly paradise, but in heaven. This is a figurative expression taken from the account of the garden of Eden, denoting the pure, satisfying, and eternal joys of heaven; and looking forward to them in this world through faith, fellowship with Christ, and the consolations of the Holy Spirit. Believers, endure your struggles here, and expect and look for a peaceful life hereafter; but not until then: the word of God never promises peace and complete freedom from conflict here.

Our Lord Jesus is the first, because all things were made through him; he existed before everything, with God, and is God himself. He is the last, because he will be the Judge of all. As this First and Last, who was dead and is alive, is the believer’s Brother and Friend, he must be rich in the deepest poverty, honorable amidst the lowest humiliation, and happy under the heaviest suffering, like the church of Smyrna. Many who are wealthy in this world are poor in the next; and some who are outwardly poor are inwardly rich—rich in faith, in good works, rich in privileges, rich in gifts, rich in hope. Where there is spiritual abundance, outward poverty can be well endured; and when God’s people are made poor for the sake of Christ and a good conscience, he makes up to them in spiritual riches. Christ provides protection against future troubles. Do not fear any of these things; not only forbid slavish fear, but subdue it, strengthening the soul with courage. It should be meant to test them, not destroy them. Note the certainty of the reward: “I will give you:” they will receive the reward from Christ's own hand. Also, note how appropriate it is: “a crown of life:” those who spend their lives serving him or die for his cause will be rewarded with a much better life, which will be eternal. The second death is unspeakably worse than the first death, both in its agony and because it is an eternal death; it is indeed terrible to die and continue dying. If someone is kept from the second death and God’s wrath, they may patiently endure whatever they experience in this world.

The word of God is a sword, able to slay both sin and sinners. It turns and cuts every way; but the believer need not fear this sword; yet this confidence cannot be supported without steady obedience. As our Lord notices all the advantages and opportunities we have for duty in the places where we dwell, so he notices our temptations and discouragements from the same causes. In a situation of trials, the church of Pergamos had not denied the faith, either by open apostasy or by compromising to avoid suffering. Christ commends their steadfastness but reproves their sinful failures. A wrong understanding of gospel doctrine and Christian liberty was a root of bitterness from which evil practices grew. Repentance is the duty of churches and groups of people, as well as individual persons; those who sin together should repent together. Here is the promise of favor to those who overcome. The influences and comforts of the Spirit of Christ come down from heaven into the soul for its support. This is hidden from the rest of the world. The new name is the name of adoption; when the Holy Spirit reveals his work in a believer’s soul, this new name and its true meaning are understood by him.

Even when the Lord knows the works of his people to be done in love, faith, zeal, and patience; yet if his eyes, which are as a flame of fire, observe them committing or allowing what is evil, he will rebuke, correct, or punish them. Here is praise of the ministry and people of Thyatira, by One who knew the principles from which they acted. They grew wiser and better. All Christians should earnestly desire that their last works may be their best works. Yet this church overlooked some wicked deceivers. God is known by the judgments he executes; and by this judgment upon deceivers, he shows his certain knowledge of the hearts of men, of their principles, designs, frame, and temper. Encouragement is given to those who kept themselves pure and undefiled. It is dangerous to despise the mystery of God, and as dangerous to receive the mysteries of Satan. Let us beware of the depths of Satan, of which those who know the least are the most happy. How tender Christ is of his faithful servants! He lays nothing upon his servants but what is for their good. There is promise of an ample reward to the persevering, victorious believer; also knowledge and wisdom, suitable to their power and dominion. Christ brings day with him into the soul, the light of grace and of glory, in the presence and enjoyment of him their Lord and Savior. After every victory let us follow up our advantage against the enemy, that we may overcome and keep the works of Christ to the end.
